file_name: Ibrahim et al. - 2023 - Enhanced skin cancer classification using pre-trained CNN models and transfer learning a clinical d.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with transfer learning, to classify skin cancer. The authors trained modified pre-trained models (EfficientNetB3, EfficientNetB0, and ResNet50) on the HAM10000 dataset of dermatoscopic images to achieve multi-class skin tumor classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the modification and training of pre-trained CNN models (EfficientNetB3, EfficientNetB0, and ResNet50) on the HAM10000 dataset. It details a specific methodology including data augmentation, class imbalance handling, and fine-tuning, and reports new quantitative results (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) comparing modified models against original ones. Although published in conference proceedings, it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Enhanced Skin Cancer Classification using Pre- Trained CNN Models and Transfer Learning: A Clinical Decision Support System for Dermatologists
journal: International Journal for Computers and Information, IJCI
brief_summary: This study aimed to enhance the classification accuracy of seven types of skin tumors using transfer learning on modified pre-trained Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models. The researchers utilized the HAM10000 dataset, which consists of 10,015 dermatoscopic images, and addressed class imbalance through data augmentation and balanced sampling techniques. Three architectures—EfficientNetB3, EfficientNetB0, and ResNet50—were fine-tuned using ImageNet pre-trained weights and evaluated on the dataset. The modified EfficientNetB3 model outperformed the other models and the original unmodified versions, achieving an accuracy of 91.6%, precision of 83%, F1-score of 88%, and recall of 94%. These results demonstrate the superiority of the proposed modified models for multi-class skin cancer classification and their potential as a clinical decision support system.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset; described as a lesion that can develop into a malignant tumor.

all_task_types.target: Seven types of skin tumors (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of dermatoscopic images into seven discrete diagnostic categories using modified pre-trained CNN models (EfficientNetB3, EfficientNetB0, ResNet50) on the HAM10000 dataset. The task involves predicting discrete lesion classes rather than continuous values.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public benchmark dataset of dermatoscopic images collected over 20 years from Cliff Rosendahl's skin cancer clinic in Australia and the Department of Dermatology at the Medical University in Austria. It contains 10,015 images with a resolution of 600x450 pixels.

use.training: Used for training the modified EfficientNetB3, EfficientNetB0, and ResNet50 models (70% split).
use.validation: Used for validation during model training (10% split).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the models (20% split).
use.other: Used as the primary benchmark dataset for the study.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between modified and original models were based on descriptive statistics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.experiment: Comparison of Original vs. Modified Pre-trained CNN Models
HAM10000.notes: The modified EfficientNetB3 model outperformed all other models. Applying imbalance handling and data augmentation increased Modified EfficientNetB3 accuracy from 87.8% to 91.6%. The modified models showed superior performance compared to original versions.

HAM10000_Class_Wise_Performance_Modified.notes: Modified EfficientNetB3 achieved the highest average precision score among all models (ranging from 0.68 to 0.98).
Modified EfficientNetB0 demonstrated moderate performance with an average precision score but achieved higher average recall scores.

Comparison_with_Previous_Works.experiment: Comparison of Accuracy with Previous Studies
Comparison_with_Previous_Works.notes: The proposed method outperformed other classification methods listed in the comparison.

architecture_used.main_model: Modified EfficientNetB3, Modified EfficientNetB0, and Modified ResNet50

architecture_details.modifications: The top three layers of the original pre-trained models (Global Average Pooling2D, dropout, and output layer) were replaced. The new head consists of: two convolutional layers with 3x3 kernel size and 128 filters each, followed by batch normalization; a global average pooling layer; a dense layer with 256 units and ReLU activation; a dropout layer with rate 0.2; and a final dense output layer with softmax activation for 7 classes.
architecture_details.EfficientNetB3_details: 25.2 million parameters, compound scaling factor 1.2, width multiplier 1.2, depth multiplier 1.1
architecture_details.ResNet50_details: 50 layers total (1 fully connected layer, 49 convolution layers), 5 convolutional layer blocks (Blocks 1-5), 1000 node fully connected layer with softmax in original architecture

training_details.data_augmentation: Rotation (0-90 degrees), zooming, horizontal and vertical flipping
training_details.imbalance_handling: Weighted loss function to increase class weight for minority classes

preprocessing_techniques: Data resizing (images resized from original 600x450 pixels to 224x224 pixels for model input)
Data augmentation (rotation of 0-90 degrees, zooming, horizontal flipping, and vertical flipping)
Balanced sampling (used to address class imbalance)
Weighted loss function (used to increase class weight for minority classes to handle imbalance)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied via Keras image data generator
Applied via Keras image data generator
Applied via Keras image data generator

conclusions: The modified pre-trained CNN models (EfficientNetB3, EfficientNetB0, and ResNet50) outperformed their original unmodified counterparts in multi-class skin cancer classification on the HAM10000 dataset.
The modified EfficientNetB3 model achieved the highest performance with an accuracy of 91.6%, precision of 83%, F1-score of 88%, and recall of 94%.

Applying modifications to the pre-trained models resulted in significant performance improvements over the original models, including a 2.8% increase in accuracy for Modified EfficientNetB3 compared to Original EfficientNetB3.

The study demonstrates that transfer learning combined with architectural modifications and techniques to handle class imbalance (data augmentation and weighted loss) is an effective approach for enhancing skin tumor classification accuracy.
The proposed modified models show potential as a clinical decision support system to aid dermatologists in diagnosing skin cancer with higher accuracy and efficiency.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which introduces potential population bias as the data was collected from only two specific sources (Cliff Rosendahl's clinic in Australia and the Medical University in Austria) over 20 years, limiting external generalizability.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with Melanocytic Nevi (NV) comprising 6705 images (67%) and Dermatofibroma (DF) comprising only 115 images (1.15%), necessitating the use of data augmentation and weighted loss functions.

The paper does not report any formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals; performance comparisons are based solely on descriptive statistics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score).

The dataset was split into 70% training, 10% validation, and 20% testing sets, but the paper does not specify if k-fold cross-validation was used, raising potential concerns about data leakage or overfitting to a specific split.

The input images were resized from their original 600x450 resolution to 224x224 pixels for model input, which may result in the loss of fine-grained diagnostic details critical for dermatological analysis.

The study is a conference paper published in a specialized journal (IJCI) rather than a high-impact clinical or medical journal, which may affect the perceived clinical relevance and rigorous peer review standards.

The proposed models were evaluated only on the HAM10000 dataset; there is no external validation on independent datasets to confirm the robustness of the 91.6% accuracy claim in diverse clinical settings.
The paper acknowledges that future work is needed on larger datasets with more labeled skin lesions, indicating current limitations in the scope and generalizability of the findings.
